---
title: "Inference"
subtitle: "Standard errors, clustering, and hypothesis testing in regression"
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
**In this section, you will learn to**

- Explain why $\hat\beta$ is a random variable whose standard error is the spread of its sampling distribution, and build the $t$-statistic and confidence interval from it
- Derive the classical variance $\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ as the homoskedastic special case of the sandwich formula, and diagnose why heteroskedasticity makes it the wrong standard error
- Apply heteroskedasticity-robust (HC1) and cluster-robust standard errors, and decide the level at which to cluster — the level at which treatment is assigned or correlation lives
- Recognize the few-clusters problem (Sweden's 21 regioner) and why cluster-robust inference needs many clusters to behave
- Distinguish the t-test from the joint F-test, use the identity $F = t^2$ for a single restriction, and connect specification searching to multiple testing
:::

## From a Point Estimate to a Claim

The previous pages of this module taught you how to *compute* a regression slope --- how OLS mechanically minimizes the sum of squared residuals, how the Frisch-Waugh-Lovell theorem strips out covariates, how omitted variables bias the estimate, and how functional form bends the fitted line. But a number on its own is not a finding. Suppose a Swedish *region* fits a regression of patients' HbA1c on the number of *distriktssköterskor* (district nurses) staffed at their *vårdcentral* and reports a slope of $-0.18$ percentage points per additional nurse (HbA1c is itself measured in percent, so an *absolute* change in it is a change in *percentage points* --- a drop from 7.0% to 6.82%, not a 0.18% relative change). Is that a real relationship, or could a slope that size have arisen from sampling noise alone, even if more nurses do nothing at all?

Answering that question is **inference**. We did the conceptual groundwork in Module 3: the [Hypothesis Testing](../03-statistical-foundations/hypothesis-testing.qmd) page built the logic of null hypotheses, $t$-statistics, and p-values, and the [Confidence Intervals](../03-statistical-foundations/confidence-intervals.qmd) page turned the binary verdict into an interval of plausible values. This page carries that machinery into the regression setting and confronts the question that decides whether your inference is honest or self-deceiving: **what is the right standard error?**

The headline messages of the page are three. First, $\hat{\beta}$ is a *random variable* --- it would come out differently in a different sample --- and its standard error measures that run-to-run spread. Second, the textbook (homoskedastic) standard error formula is fragile: it breaks the moment the error variance is not constant, which in real health data it almost never is. Third, when observations cluster --- patients within a vårdcentral, repeated measurements within a region --- ignoring that structure makes standard errors far too small and routinely manufactures "significant" results out of thin air.


## $\hat{\beta}$ Is a Random Variable

Consider the simple regression model

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + u_i,
$$

where $Y_i$ is patient $i$'s HbA1c, $X_i$ is the staffing level at their vårdcentral, and $u_i$ is everything else that affects HbA1c. We never see the true $\beta_1$. We see a *sample*, and from it OLS produces an estimate $\hat{\beta}_1$.

The key realization is that $\hat{\beta}_1$ inherits randomness from the sample. Draw a different set of patients --- a different random sample from the same population --- and the regression line tilts to a slightly different slope. The collection of all the slopes you would get across all possible samples is the **sampling distribution** of $\hat{\beta}_1$. Its center is (under the assumptions of unbiasedness) the true $\beta_1$; its spread is the standard error.

::: {.glass-card .glass-card--definition}
The **standard error** of $\hat{\beta}_1$, written $\operatorname{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)$, is the standard deviation of its sampling distribution:

$$
\operatorname{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1)}.
$$

**How to read this**: "The standard error is the square root of the variance of the estimator --- it is the typical amount by which $\hat{\beta}_1$ would bounce around from one random sample to the next." A small SE means the slope is pinned down precisely; a large SE means it is barely distinguishable from noise.
:::

Once we have a standard error, the testing apparatus from Module 3 transfers directly. To test the null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_1 = b_0$ (most often $b_0 = 0$, "staffing has no association with HbA1c"), we form the **t-statistic**

$$
t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1 - b_0}{\operatorname{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)},
$$

which counts how many standard errors separate our estimate from the null value. And the **95% confidence interval** is

$$
\hat{\beta}_1 \pm t^{*} \cdot \operatorname{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1),
$$

where $t^{*}$ is the critical value (about $1.96$ in large samples, slightly larger in small ones). **Read this as**: "the set of slopes that we would *not* reject at the 5% level --- our honest range of plausible values for the true effect." Everything on this page is ultimately about getting $\operatorname{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)$ right, because a wrong standard error poisons the $t$-statistic, the p-value, and the confidence interval all at once.


## The Classical Variance Formula --- and Why It Breaks

Under the classical regression assumptions, the variance of the OLS coefficient vector has a famously compact form. Collect the regressors in the matrix $X$ and the errors in the vector $u$. The classical assumption is **homoskedasticity**: every error has the same variance $\sigma^2$, and errors are uncorrelated with one another. Formally, $\operatorname{Var}(u \mid X) = \sigma^2 I$, where $I$ is the identity matrix.

::: {.glass-card .glass-card--assumption}
**Homoskedasticity and the classical variance.** If $\operatorname{Var}(u_i \mid X) = \sigma^2$ for every $i$ (constant variance) and the errors are mutually uncorrelated, then

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta} \mid X) = \sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1}.
$$

**How to read this**: "The variance of the whole coefficient vector equals the common error variance $\sigma^2$ multiplied by the inverse of the cross-product matrix $X'X$." The diagonal entries of this matrix are the variances of the individual coefficients; their square roots are the classical standard errors that `lm()` prints by default. In the single-regressor case it simplifies to the familiar $\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{\sum_i (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$.
:::

The formula is elegant, but it rests entirely on that one word **homoskedasticity** --- equal variance for all observations. In Swedish primary-care data this assumption is almost always false. Picture HbA1c against vårdcentral size. Tiny rural centers with a handful of diabetics show wildly variable average HbA1c; large urban centers with thousands of patients show tightly clustered averages. The spread of the errors *depends on $X$*. That is **heteroskedasticity**: non-constant error variance, $\operatorname{Var}(u_i \mid X_i) = \sigma_i^2$, with the $\sigma_i^2$ differing across observations.

When errors are heteroskedastic, $\sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1}$ is simply the *wrong* variance. It can be too big or too small, but in the most common patterns --- variance rising with $X$ --- it is too *small*, which makes the classical $t$-statistic too *big* and the confidence interval too *narrow*. You reject the null more often than you should. Crucially, OLS itself is still unbiased under heteroskedasticity (the point estimate $\hat{\beta}_1$ is fine); it is only the *inference* that is broken. The fix is not to abandon OLS but to fix the standard error.

The two pictures below show the difference the prose describes. Both share the *same* fitted line; what changes is the vertical scatter of the points around it. On the left the spread is constant (homoskedastic); on the right it fans out as vårdcentral size grows --- small rural centers scatter wildly, large urban centers hug the line. The right-hand pattern is what makes the classical SE the wrong one.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

// Self-contained illustration of homoskedastic vs heteroskedastic errors.
// Same true line on both panels; only the error spread differs. Pure: depends
// only on d3. No mutable state, no link to the live coverage widget below.
p2_scatterData = {
  const rng = d3.randomLcg(42);           // seeded so the figure is stable
  const rnorm = d3.randomNormal.source(rng)(0, 1);
  const runif = d3.randomUniform.source(rng)(0, 4);
  const b0 = 2.0, b1 = 0.5, n = 140;
  const rows = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const x = runif();
    const e = rnorm();
    rows.push({
      x,
      yHomo: b0 + b1 * x + e * 1.1,                 // constant spread
      yHetero: b0 + b1 * x + e * (0.25 + 0.9 * x)   // spread grows with x
    });
  }
  return rows;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Two side-by-side panels sharing one fitted line and y-domain.
{
  const panelW = Math.min(300, (width - 24) / 2);
  const mkPanel = (yfield, title, note) => Plot.plot({
    width: panelW,
    height: 250,
    marginLeft: 46,
    marginBottom: 46,
    marginTop: 34,
    style: { fontFamily: "'Inter', -apple-system, sans-serif", fontSize: "11px", color: "#1c1c1a", background: "transparent" },
    x: { label: "Vårdcentral size (x) →", domain: [0, 4], grid: true },
    y: { label: "↑ HbA1c (y)", domain: [-1.5, 7.5], grid: true },
    marks: [
      Plot.dot(p2_scatterData, { x: "x", y: yfield, fill: "#3d5a6c", fillOpacity: 0.45, r: 2.6 }),
      Plot.linearRegressionY(p2_scatterData, { x: "x", y: yfield, stroke: "#990000", strokeWidth: 2.5, ci: 0 }),
      Plot.text([{}], {
        frameAnchor: "top", dy: -18, text: [title],
        fill: "#1c1c1a", fontWeight: 700, fontSize: 12,
        stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 3, paintOrder: "stroke"
      }),
      Plot.text([{}], {
        frameAnchor: "bottom", dy: -4, text: [note],
        fill: "#79766e", fontWeight: 600, fontSize: 10,
        stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 3, paintOrder: "stroke"
      })
    ]
  });
  return html`<div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:1rem;justify-content:center;align-items:flex-start;">
    ${mkPanel("yHomo", "Homoskedastic", "constant spread — classical SE is right")}
    ${mkPanel("yHetero", "Heteroskedastic", "spread fans out with x — classical SE wrong")}
  </div>`;
}
```

::: {.aside}
Left: errors have the same variance everywhere, so the classical formula $\sigma^2(X'X)^{-1}$ is correct. Right: error variance grows with $x$ (the rural-vs-urban vårdcentral picture) --- the classical SE understates uncertainty where the points spread out, and a robust SE is needed to repair it.
:::


## The Sandwich: Heteroskedasticity-Robust Standard Errors

The general expression for the variance of OLS, valid whether or not errors are homoskedastic, is the **sandwich formula**:

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta} \mid X) = \underbrace{(X'X)^{-1}}_{\text{bread}} \; \underbrace{\Big(X' \,\Omega\, X\Big)}_{\text{meat}} \; \underbrace{(X'X)^{-1}}_{\text{bread}},
$$

where $\Omega = \operatorname{Var}(u \mid X)$ is the (unknown) error covariance matrix. **Read this as**: "the variance equals bread, times meat, times bread" --- the two outer $(X'X)^{-1}$ factors are the bread, the middle $X'\Omega X$ is the meat.

The classical formula is the special case where the meat collapses: if $\Omega = \sigma^2 I$, then $X'\Omega X = \sigma^2 X'X$, the middle and one slice of bread cancel, and you are left with $\sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1}$. The sandwich is the parent; the classical formula is its homoskedastic child.

The practical problem is that we do not know $\Omega$. Halbert White's (1980) insight was that we do not need to know each $\sigma_i^2$ individually --- we only need to estimate the *meat*, and for that the squared OLS residual $\hat{u}_i^2$ is a serviceable (if noisy) stand-in for $\sigma_i^2$. This yields the **heteroskedasticity-consistent (HC)** estimator, universally called **"robust" standard errors**:

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(\hat{\beta}) = (X'X)^{-1}\left(\sum_{i} \hat{u}_i^2 \, x_i x_i' \right)(X'X)^{-1}.
$$

Two pieces of notation worth unpacking here. The term $x_i x_i'$ (an **outer product**) is just the little matrix you get by multiplying one observation's row of regressors by itself; summed up, it rebuilds $X'X$, and weighting each term by $\hat{u}_i^2$ lets observations with bigger residuals count for more in the variance. And although no single squared residual $\hat{u}_i^2$ can estimate that observation's variance $\sigma_i^2$ on its own, the *sum* is well-behaved --- the over- and under-shoots average out, so the whole meat is estimated consistently even though each piece is noisy.

In finite samples the raw version underestimates the variance, so software applies a small correction. The default in Stata and the most common report in applied work is **HC1**, which multiplies the meat by $n/(n-k)$ (sample size over residual degrees of freedom). Variants HC2 and HC3 use leverage-based corrections and behave better in small samples; HC3 is the most conservative.

::: {.glass-card .glass-card--insight}
**Modern default: report robust standard errors.** Because robust SEs are valid under heteroskedasticity *and* coincide with classical SEs when errors happen to be homoskedastic (in large samples), there is essentially no downside to using them and a large downside to not. The cost is a slightly noisier variance estimate in very small samples. Contemporary applied econometrics --- and the editorial norms of health-economics journals --- treats robust (or clustered, see below) standard errors as the baseline, and classical SEs as the thing you must *justify*.
:::

When will robust and classical SEs differ noticeably? When heteroskedasticity is strong *and* it is correlated with the regressor of interest. If the error variance is genuinely flat, the two agree and you have lost nothing. If it is not, the robust SE corrects an error the classical one would have hidden. The interactive below lets you watch this happen.


## See It: Confidence-Interval Coverage Under Heteroskedasticity

A 95% confidence interval makes a promise: across many repeated samples, 95% of the intervals it produces should contain the true $\beta_1$. That fraction is called the **coverage**. A well-calibrated procedure has coverage near 95%; a broken one under-covers. The simulation below draws many samples from a known model where the true slope is exactly $\beta_1 = 0.5$, builds a confidence interval from each sample using *either* classical *or* robust standard errors, and tracks how often the interval actually catches the truth.

::: {.interactive-container}

#### Monte Carlo coverage: classical vs robust CIs

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Start here:** Begin with the sliders at γ = 0 and SE type = Classical to see a well-calibrated 95% interval, then drag γ up to break it and switch to Robust to repair it.
:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof inf_hetero = Inputs.range([0, 2.5], {
  value: 0,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "Heteroskedasticity strength (γ)"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

html`<div style="font-family:'Inter',-apple-system,sans-serif;font-size:0.72rem;color:#79766e;margin:-0.4rem 0 0.6rem 0;">γ = 0 means constant error variance (homoskedastic); higher γ makes the errors fan out more strongly with x.</div>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof inf_setype = Inputs.radio(["Classical", "Robust (HC1)"], {
  value: "Classical",
  label: "Standard error type"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof inf_nobs = Inputs.range([20, 200], {
  value: 60,
  step: 10,
  label: "Sample size per simulation (n)"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof inf_draw = Inputs.button("Reshuffle random draws", { value: 0, reduce: v => v + 1 })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

html`<div style="font-family:'Inter',-apple-system,sans-serif;font-size:0.72rem;color:#79766e;margin:-0.4rem 0 0.6rem 0;">The chart updates live as you drag the sliders (they change the experiment); this button only redraws the random sample at the current settings.</div>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

// Truth and model constants (one definition per cell)
inf_truth = 0.5          // true slope beta_1
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

inf_intercept = 2.0      // true intercept
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

inf_zcrit = 1.96         // large-sample 95% critical value
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

// Simulate ONE dataset, fit OLS, return CI using both classical and robust SEs.
// Heteroskedasticity: sd of the error grows with x as (1 + gamma * x).
inf_simulate = function (n, gamma) {
  const rnorm = d3.randomNormal(0, 1);
  const runif = d3.randomUniform(0, 4);   // x in [0, 4]
  const xs = d3.range(n).map(() => runif());
  const xbar = d3.mean(xs);
  const ys = xs.map(x => {
    const sd = 1 + gamma * x;             // error sd depends on x -> heteroskedastic
    return inf_intercept + inf_truth * x + rnorm() * sd;
  });

  // OLS slope and intercept
  const Sxx = d3.sum(xs, x => (x - xbar) ** 2);
  const ybar = d3.mean(ys);
  const Sxy = d3.sum(xs.map((x, i) => (x - xbar) * (ys[i] - ybar)));
  const b1 = Sxy / Sxx;
  const b0 = ybar - b1 * xbar;

  // Residuals
  const resid = xs.map((x, i) => ys[i] - (b0 + b1 * x));
  const k = 2;

  // Classical SE: sigma^2 / Sxx, with sigma^2 = RSS/(n-k)
  const rss = d3.sum(resid, e => e * e);
  const sigma2 = rss / (n - k);
  const seClassical = Math.sqrt(sigma2 / Sxx);

  // Robust HC1 SE for the slope: sandwich with finite-sample correction
  const meat = d3.sum(xs.map((x, i) => (x - xbar) ** 2 * resid[i] ** 2));
  const varRobust = (n / (n - k)) * meat / (Sxx * Sxx);
  const seRobust = Math.sqrt(varRobust);

  return { b1, seClassical, seRobust };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

// Build a batch of simulations, attaching CIs and a "covers" flag for the
// currently selected SE type.
inf_makeBatch = function (count, n, gamma, seType) {
  const out = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    const s = inf_simulate(n, gamma);
    const se = seType === "Classical" ? s.seClassical : s.seRobust;
    const lo = s.b1 - inf_zcrit * se;
    const hi = s.b1 + inf_zcrit * se;
    out.push({
      b1: s.b1,
      lo,
      hi,
      covers: lo <= inf_truth && inf_truth <= hi
    });
  }
  return out;
};
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| output: false

// A fresh batch of 500 simulated regressions at the CURRENT settings. This cell
// re-runs whenever a slider or the SE-type radio changes (so coverage responds
// live as you drag) and whenever "Reshuffle random draws" is clicked (which reshuffles
// the random draws at the current settings). No accumulation, no mutable state —
// each draw is a self-contained Monte Carlo experiment.
inf_results = {
  inf_draw;   // take a dependency on the button so each click redraws
  return inf_makeBatch(500, inf_nobs, inf_hetero, inf_setype);
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Coverage summary cards
{
  const res = inf_results;
  const nTot = res.length;
  const nCover = res.filter(d => d.covers).length;
  const cov = nTot > 0 ? (nCover / nTot * 100) : null;
  const ok = cov !== null && cov >= 93 && cov <= 97;
  const covColor = cov === null ? "#79766e" : (ok ? "#2e6f40" : "#b3000b");

  return html`
  <div style="display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(8.5rem,1fr));gap:0.5rem;margin-bottom:0.4rem;font-family:'Inter',-apple-system,sans-serif;font-size:0.82rem;">
    <div style="min-height:3.4rem;padding:0.55rem 0.8rem;border-radius:0.5rem;background:#f6f5f1;border:1px solid #e6e3db;">
      <div style="font-weight:600;color:#79766e;font-size:0.65rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.04em;">Samples</div>
      <div style="font-size:1.25rem;font-weight:700;color:#1c1c1a;">${nTot}</div>
    </div>
    <div style="min-height:3.4rem;padding:0.55rem 0.8rem;border-radius:0.5rem;background:rgba(153,0,0,0.06);border:1px solid rgba(153,0,0,0.18);">
      <div style="font-weight:600;color:#79766e;font-size:0.65rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.04em;">SE type · γ</div>
      <div style="font-size:1.1rem;font-weight:700;color:#990000;">${inf_setype.split(" ")[0]} · ${inf_hetero.toFixed(1)}</div>
    </div>
    <div style="min-height:3.4rem;padding:0.55rem 0.8rem;border-radius:0.5rem;background:${cov===null?'#f6f5f1':(ok?'rgba(46,111,64,0.08)':'rgba(179,0,11,0.06)')};border:1px solid ${cov===null?'#e6e3db':(ok?'rgba(46,111,64,0.22)':'rgba(179,0,11,0.18)')};">
      <div style="font-weight:600;color:#79766e;font-size:0.65rem;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:0.04em;">Coverage</div>
      <div style="font-size:1.25rem;font-weight:700;color:${covColor};">${cov===null?'—':cov.toFixed(1)+'%'}</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div style="font-family:'Inter',-apple-system,sans-serif;font-size:0.72rem;color:#79766e;margin-bottom:0.75rem;">
    Target coverage is 95%. <span style="color:#2e6f40;font-weight:600;">Green</span> = within 93–97% (well-calibrated); <span style="color:#b3000b;font-weight:600;">red</span> = outside that band.
  </div>`;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Interval-bar plot: show the most recent 60 intervals, colored by coverage
{
  const res = inf_results;
  if (res.length === 0) {
    return html`<div style="text-align:center;color:#79766e;padding:1.5rem 0;font-family:'Inter',-apple-system,sans-serif;font-size:0.9rem;">The chart updates live as you drag the controls. Each horizontal bar is one sample's 95% CI; green bars catch the true slope (0.5), red bars miss it.</div>`;
  }
  const recent = res.slice(-60).map((d, i) => ({ ...d, row: i }));
  return Plot.plot({
    width: Math.min(640, width),
    height: 320,
    marginLeft: 30,
    marginTop: 30,
    style: { fontFamily: "'Inter', -apple-system, sans-serif", fontSize: "12px", color: "#1c1c1a", background: "transparent" },
    x: { label: "Estimated slope (95% CI)", domain: [-0.6, 1.6], grid: true },
    y: { label: null, ticks: [] },
    color: {
      domain: ["Covers truth", "Misses truth"],
      range: ["#2e6f40", "#b3000b"],
      legend: true
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleX([inf_truth], { stroke: "#1c1c1a", strokeWidth: 2, strokeDasharray: "5,4" }),
      Plot.link(recent, {
        x1: "lo", x2: "hi", y: "row",
        stroke: d => d.covers ? "Covers truth" : "Misses truth",
        strokeWidth: 1.6, strokeOpacity: 0.78
      }),
      Plot.dot(recent, { x: "b1", y: "row", fill: d => d.covers ? "Covers truth" : "Misses truth", r: 2 }),
      Plot.text([{}], {
        frameAnchor: "top", dx: 0, dy: -14,
        text: ["true β₁ = 0.5"], fill: "#1c1c1a",
        fontWeight: 600, fontSize: 11,
        stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 3, paintOrder: "stroke"
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Histogram of the slope estimates (its sampling distribution)
{
  const res = inf_results;
  if (res.length === 0) return html``;
  return Plot.plot({
    width: Math.min(640, width),
    height: 200,
    marginLeft: 50,
    style: { fontFamily: "'Inter', -apple-system, sans-serif", fontSize: "12px", color: "#1c1c1a", background: "transparent" },
    x: { label: "β̂₁ across samples (its sampling distribution)", domain: [-0.6, 1.6], grid: true },
    y: { label: "Count", grid: true },
    marks: [
      Plot.rectY(res, Plot.binX({ y: "count" }, { x: "b1", thresholds: 40, fill: "#990000", fillOpacity: 0.45 })),
      Plot.ruleX([inf_truth], { stroke: "#1c1c1a", strokeWidth: 2, strokeDasharray: "5,4" }),
      Plot.ruleY([0])
    ]
  });
}
```

:::

### How to Read the Coverage Demo

Walk through this in order; each step isolates one idea.

**Step 1 --- A calibrated baseline.** Set the heteroskedasticity strength $\gamma = 0$, leave the SE type on **Classical**, and keep $n = 60$. With no heteroskedasticity the classical formula is exactly right: the "Coverage" card sits close to **95%** (and shows green), and in the interval plot the vast majority of bars are green (they cross the dashed line at the true $\beta_1 = 0.5$). About 1 in 20 bars is red --- the 5% of intervals that legitimately miss, exactly as the 95% promise allows. Click **Reshuffle random draws** a few times and watch the number jitter around 95% --- that jitter is sampling noise in the coverage estimate itself.

**Step 2 --- Turn on heteroskedasticity, keep classical SEs.** Drag $\gamma$ up to around **1.4**, still using **Classical** standard errors. The coverage card responds immediately: it slips well below 95% --- typically into the high 80s --- and more red bars appear. The intervals are systematically *too narrow*: the classical formula assumed a single error variance, but here the errors fan out with $x$, so it under-states the true uncertainty and the intervals miss the truth too often.

**Step 3 --- Switch to robust standard errors.** Keep $\gamma = 1.4$ and switch the radio to **Robust (HC1)**. The point estimates $\hat{\beta}_1$ are unchanged --- OLS is unbiased either way, so the histogram of slopes looks the same. What changes is the *width* of every interval: robust SEs widen them just enough to account for the fanning errors. Coverage climbs back toward **95%** and the green bars return. You have watched a broken inference get repaired without touching the point estimate.

**Step 4 --- Sample size does not save classical SEs.** Set $\gamma = 1.4$ and **Classical**, then push $n$ up to 200. Coverage stays stuck below 95% no matter how large $n$ gets, and clicking **Reshuffle random draws** only reshuffles *which* intervals miss --- it never repairs the rate. This is the crucial lesson: heteroskedasticity bias in the standard error does **not** vanish with more data. Only the right formula fixes it. (The slope histogram does get tighter --- more data means a more precise estimate --- but a precise estimate paired with a wrong SE is just a confident mistake.)


## Clustered Standard Errors

Heteroskedasticity breaks the *equal-variance* part of the classical assumption. Clustering breaks the other part: the assumption that errors are **uncorrelated across observations**.

In Swedish primary-care data, observations almost never arrive independently. Patients are nested inside *vårdcentraler*; vårdcentraler are nested inside *regioner*. Two diabetics at the same vårdcentral share a physician, a triage protocol, a local socioeconomic environment, and a building --- so their unobserved determinants of HbA1c are *correlated*. When you regress HbA1c on a vårdcentral-level program indicator, the errors within a center move together. Treating 8,000 correlated patients as 8,000 independent draws vastly overstates how much independent information you actually have.

::: {.glass-card .glass-card--warning}
**Within-group correlation makes classical (and even robust) SEs too small.** If the treatment is assigned at the group level and errors are correlated within groups, the effective number of independent observations is closer to the number of *groups* than the number of *individuals*. Ignoring this produces standard errors that can be a small fraction of their correct size, $t$-statistics several times too large, and confidence intervals far too narrow --- the single most common way that observational health studies overstate their precision.
:::

The picture below makes the nested structure --- and what it does to the error covariance matrix $\Omega$ --- concrete. On the left, patients (dots) sit inside vårdcentraler (boxes), which sit inside regioner; patients in the same center share unobserved influences, so their errors move together. On the right is the matrix $\Omega = \operatorname{Var}(u \mid X)$: each shaded block is one center, where every pair of patients may be correlated (off-diagonal colour); the white space between blocks is the assumption that *different* centers are independent. Clustering sums the meat over those shaded blocks instead of over single points.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Static nesting + block-diagonal Omega figure. Pure: depends only on d3.
// Left panel = regioner ⊃ vårdcentraler ⊃ patients; right panel = the
// block-diagonal error covariance Omega the clustering estimator assumes.
{
  const W = Math.min(560, width);
  const H = 250;
  const svg = d3.create("svg")
    .attr("width", W).attr("height", H)
    .attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("font-family", "'Inter', sans-serif")
    .style("max-width", "100%").style("height", "auto");

  const half = W / 2;

  // ── LEFT: nesting diagram ──
  const L = svg.append("g");
  L.append("text").attr("x", half / 2).attr("y", 18)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", 12).attr("font-weight", 700)
    .attr("fill", "#1c1c1a").text("Nested data: region ⊃ center ⊃ patient");

  // Region outer box
  L.append("rect").attr("x", 16).attr("y", 30).attr("width", half - 32).attr("height", H - 56)
    .attr("rx", 8).attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#3d5a6c").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  L.append("text").attr("x", 24).attr("y", 46).attr("font-size", 10).attr("font-weight", 700)
    .attr("fill", "#3d5a6c").text("Region");

  // Two center boxes, each with patient dots that "move together"
  const centerW = (half - 64) / 2;
  const centers = [0, 1];
  for (const c of centers) {
    const bx = 28 + c * (centerW + 16);
    const by = 56;
    const bw = centerW, bh = H - 100;
    L.append("rect").attr("x", bx).attr("y", by).attr("width", bw).attr("height", bh)
      .attr("rx", 6).attr("fill", "#f6f5f1").attr("stroke", "#990000").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
    L.append("text").attr("x", bx + bw / 2).attr("y", by + 14)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", 9).attr("font-weight", 600)
      .attr("fill", "#990000").text(`Vårdcentral ${c + 1}`);
    // patient dots, tinted per center to signal "correlated within"
    const tint = c === 0 ? "#990000" : "#9c7a33";
    for (let i = 0; i < 6; i++) {
      const px = bx + 14 + (i % 3) * ((bw - 28) / 2);
      const py = by + 34 + Math.floor(i / 3) * 26;
      L.append("circle").attr("cx", px).attr("cy", py).attr("r", 5)
        .attr("fill", tint).attr("fill-opacity", 0.55).attr("stroke", tint).attr("stroke-width", 1);
    }
  }
  L.append("text").attr("x", half / 2).attr("y", H - 8)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", 9).attr("fill", "#79766e")
    .text("patients in a center share errors (same colour)");

  // ── RIGHT: block-diagonal Omega ──
  const R = svg.append("g").attr("transform", `translate(${half},0)`);
  R.append("text").attr("x", half / 2).attr("y", 18)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", 12).attr("font-weight", 700)
    .attr("fill", "#1c1c1a").text("Ω = Var(u | X): block-diagonal");

  const gx = 40, gy = 38, cell = (H - 90) / 6;   // 6×6 grid, two 3×3 blocks
  for (let i = 0; i < 6; i++) {
    for (let j = 0; j < 6; j++) {
      const sameBlock = Math.floor(i / 3) === Math.floor(j / 3);
      const onDiag = i === j;
      let fill = "#ffffff";
      if (onDiag) fill = "#3d5a6c";
      else if (sameBlock) fill = "rgba(61,90,108,0.32)";
      R.append("rect")
        .attr("x", gx + j * cell).attr("y", gy + i * cell)
        .attr("width", cell - 1).attr("height", cell - 1)
        .attr("fill", fill).attr("stroke", "#e6e3db").attr("stroke-width", 0.5);
    }
  }
  // Block outlines
  for (const b of [0, 1]) {
    R.append("rect")
      .attr("x", gx + b * 3 * cell).attr("y", gy + b * 3 * cell)
      .attr("width", 3 * cell).attr("height", 3 * cell)
      .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#990000").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  }
  // Legend
  const ly = gy + 6 * cell + 16;
  R.append("rect").attr("x", gx).attr("y", ly).attr("width", 11).attr("height", 11).attr("fill", "#3d5a6c");
  R.append("text").attr("x", gx + 16).attr("y", ly + 9).attr("font-size", 9).attr("fill", "#79766e").text("variance (diagonal)");
  R.append("rect").attr("x", gx).attr("y", ly + 16).attr("width", 11).attr("height", 11).attr("fill", "rgba(61,90,108,0.32)");
  R.append("text").attr("x", gx + 16).attr("y", ly + 25).attr("font-size", 9).attr("fill", "#79766e").text("within-center correlation");
  R.append("rect").attr("x", gx).attr("y", ly + 32).attr("width", 11).attr("height", 11).attr("fill", "#ffffff").attr("stroke", "#e6e3db");
  R.append("text").attr("x", gx + 16).attr("y", ly + 41).attr("font-size", 9).attr("fill", "#79766e").text("zero (different centers independent)");

  return svg.node();
}
```

::: {.aside}
The cluster-robust estimator assumes the error covariance $\Omega$ is *block-diagonal*: dense blocks (each one center) on the diagonal, zeros off it. It corrects the within-center correlation (the shaded off-diagonals) while still treating different centers as independent.
:::

The cluster-robust variance estimator generalizes the sandwich. Instead of summing over individual squared residuals, you sum over *groups* of stacked residuals, allowing arbitrary correlation **within** each group while assuming independence **between** groups:

$$
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}_{\text{cluster}}(\hat{\beta}) = (X'X)^{-1}\left(\sum_{g=1}^{G} X_g' \,\hat{u}_g \hat{u}_g'\, X_g \right)(X'X)^{-1},
$$

where $g = 1, \dots, G$ indexes clusters, $X_g$ stacks the regressor rows for cluster $g$, and $\hat{u}_g$ stacks its residuals. **Read this as**: "same bread, but the meat now lets every pair of observations inside a cluster be correlated, while observations in different clusters stay independent." Heteroskedasticity-robust SEs are the special case $G = n$ (every observation is its own cluster).

### At what level do you cluster?

The governing rule is simple and strict: **cluster at the level at which treatment is assigned, or at which the correlation lives.** If a diabetes coordination program is rolled out vårdcentral-by-vårdcentral, the natural clustering is the vårdcentral. If a policy is set at the regional level, cluster on region. Clustering too *finely* (e.g., on the patient when treatment varies by center) leaves within-center correlation uncorrected; clustering at the right, coarser level fixes it.

### The few-clusters problem

There is a catch, and in the Swedish context it is acute. The cluster-robust estimator is only justified asymptotically *in the number of clusters $G$* --- it needs *many* clusters to behave. Sweden has exactly **21 regioner**. Cluster on region and you have $G = 21$, which is firmly in "too few clusters" territory: the cluster-robust SE is itself badly estimated and downward-biased, so $t$-statistics are still too large. The standard rules of thumb (you want at least 30--50 clusters) are violated. Remedies include the **wild cluster bootstrap** (Cameron, Gelbach & Miller 2008) and small-sample degrees-of-freedom corrections. We flag the problem here and develop the panel-data tools that often sidestep it in the next module.


## The t-test and the F-test

With a correct standard error in hand, two classic tests cover almost all of applied work.

The **t-test** evaluates a hypothesis about a *single* coefficient. To test $H_0: \beta_j = 0$ you compute $t = \hat{\beta}_j / \operatorname{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j)$ and compare it to the critical value, exactly as in Module 3. This answers "does *this one* regressor matter, holding the others fixed?"

The **F-test** evaluates a *joint* hypothesis about *several* coefficients at once. Suppose your staffing model includes three different staff categories --- *läkare* (physicians), *distriktssköterskor* (district nurses), and administrative staff --- and you want to ask "do staffing levels matter *at all*?" That is the joint null $H_0: \beta_{\text{läk}} = \beta_{\text{ssk}} = \beta_{\text{admin}} = 0$. The F-statistic compares the residual sum of squares of the *restricted* model (which drops all three) with the *unrestricted* model (which keeps them):

$$
F = \frac{(\mathrm{RSS}_{\text{restricted}} - \mathrm{RSS}_{\text{unrestricted}})/q}{\mathrm{RSS}_{\text{unrestricted}}/(n - k)},
$$

where the **restricted model** is the one with the tested coefficients forced to zero (the smaller model), the **unrestricted model** keeps them, $q$ is the number of restrictions (the count of coefficients you zeroed --- here 3), and $n - k$ is the **residual degrees of freedom** (observations minus the parameters the unrestricted model estimates --- the information left over after fitting). **Read this as**: "how much does the fit deteriorate, per restriction, when we force the coefficients to zero --- scaled by the residual variance we have left over?" A large $F$ means dropping those coefficients hurts the fit a lot, so they are jointly significant.

::: {.glass-card .glass-card--insight}
**The F-test is not redundant with running several t-tests.** Three coefficients can each be individually insignificant yet *jointly* highly significant --- a common pattern when regressors are correlated (collinear), so each steals explanatory power from the others. Conversely, with enough individual tests one may turn up "significant" by chance even when the group adds nothing (the multiple-testing trap). A useful identity: with a *single* restriction ($q = 1$), $F = t^2$ exactly --- the F-test reduces to the squared t-test, so the two agree for one coefficient and only diverge for joint hypotheses.
:::

The $F = t^2$ identity is easiest to *see* as a parabola: read a $t$ off the horizontal axis, square it, and you land on the $F$ that a single-restriction F-test would report. The two familiar thresholds line up too --- $t = \pm 1.96$ (the 5% two-sided t-cutoff) maps to $F \approx 3.84$ (the 5% cutoff of the $F_{1,\infty}$ distribution).

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// F = t^2 for a single restriction (q = 1). Pure static figure: depends only
// on Plot/d3. Marks the t = ±1.96 ⇒ F ≈ 3.84 correspondence.
{
  const ts = d3.range(-3.2, 3.21, 0.05).map(t => ({ t, F: t * t }));
  return Plot.plot({
    width: Math.min(520, width),
    height: 280,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 46,
    style: { fontFamily: "'Inter', -apple-system, sans-serif", fontSize: "12px", color: "#1c1c1a", background: "transparent" },
    x: { label: "t-statistic", domain: [-3.2, 3.2], grid: true },
    y: { label: "F = t²", domain: [0, 10], grid: true },
    marks: [
      // the 5% F cutoff line
      Plot.ruleY([3.84], { stroke: "#79766e", strokeWidth: 1.2, strokeDasharray: "4,4" }),
      // the t = ±1.96 verticals
      Plot.ruleX([-1.96, 1.96], { stroke: "#9c7a33", strokeWidth: 1.2, strokeDasharray: "4,4" }),
      Plot.line(ts, { x: "t", y: "F", stroke: "#990000", strokeWidth: 2.5 }),
      Plot.dot([{ t: 1.96, F: 3.84 }, { t: -1.96, F: 3.84 }], { x: "t", y: "F", fill: "#990000", r: 4 }),
      Plot.text([{ t: 0.05, F: 4.4 }], {
        x: "t", y: "F", text: ["F ≈ 3.84 (5% cutoff)"],
        fill: "#79766e", fontSize: 11, fontWeight: 600, textAnchor: "start",
        stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 3, paintOrder: "stroke"
      }),
      Plot.text([{ t: 1.96, F: 3.84 }], {
        x: "t", y: "F", dy: -12, dx: 6, text: ["t = 1.96"],
        fill: "#9c7a33", fontSize: 11, fontWeight: 700, textAnchor: "start",
        stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 3, paintOrder: "stroke"
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

::: {.aside}
For one restriction, the F-statistic is exactly the squared t-statistic, so the F-test and the (two-sided) t-test give identical p-values. The correspondence breaks for joint hypotheses ($q > 1$), where $F$ aggregates evidence across several coefficients and no single $t$ reproduces it.
:::


## p-hacking and Multiple Testing, Revisited

Getting the standard error right is necessary but not sufficient for honest inference. The [Hypothesis Testing](../03-statistical-foundations/hypothesis-testing.qmd) page warned that running many tests inflates the chance of a false positive: with $k$ independent tests at $\alpha = 0.05$, the probability of at least one spurious "significant" result is $1 - 0.95^k$, which reaches about 64% at $k = 20$. Regression makes this temptation worse, because a single dataset offers a near-infinite menu of specifications.

::: {.glass-card .glass-card--warning}
**Specification searching is multiple testing in disguise.** Trying twelve outcome variables, four control sets, three functional forms, and two subsamples, then reporting only the regression where $p < 0.05$, is exactly the multiple-comparisons problem --- but hidden, because the discarded regressions never appear in the paper. The reported p-value is then meaningless: it was selected *to be* small. Pre-register your specification, report all the regressions you ran, or apply a correction (Bonferroni, Benjamini-Hochberg). A correct standard error on a cherry-picked regression is still a lie.
:::

This is why the cluster-and-robust discipline of this page and the multiple-testing discipline of Module 3 are two halves of the same commitment: report uncertainty honestly, both *within* a regression (the right SE) and *across* the regressions you chose not to show (no p-hacking).


## A Swedish Example: Region vs. Vårdcentral Clustering

Make the clustering decision concrete. A national evaluation studies whether a diabetes coordination program lowers HbA1c. The program was rolled out **vårdcentral by vårdcentral**, and the dataset has roughly 8,000 patients spread over about 600 vårdcentraler in the 21 regioner. We regress patient HbA1c on a program indicator and a few covariates. The point estimate is the same regardless of how we compute the standard error --- say $\hat{\beta}_{\text{program}} = -0.30$ percentage points. What changes is the SE, and with it the verdict:

| Standard error type | Clusters $G$ | $\operatorname{SE}$ | $t$ | Verdict |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Classical (homoskedastic) | --- | 0.05 | $-6.0$ | "Highly significant" |
| Robust (HC1) | $n$ = 8000 | 0.07 | $-4.3$ | Still significant |
| Clustered by vårdcentral | 600 | 0.11 | $-2.7$ | Significant, honest |
| Clustered by region | 21 | 0.17 | $-1.8$ | **Not** significant |

The forest plot below draws the same four rows as 95% confidence intervals around the single point estimate $\hat\beta = -0.30$. The estimate never moves; only the interval grows as we admit more of the correlation we had been ignoring. Watch it balloon from top to bottom --- and notice that only the region-clustered interval (bottom) finally crosses the dashed line at zero.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Forest plot of the four SE choices for the Swedish example. Pure static
// figure built from the table's numbers; intervals = beta ± 1.96·SE.
{
  const beta = -0.30;
  const z = 1.96;
  const rows = [
    { label: "Classical", se: 0.05, order: 4 },
    { label: "Robust (HC1)", se: 0.07, order: 3 },
    { label: "Clustered by vårdcentral", se: 0.11, order: 2 },
    { label: "Clustered by region (G=21)", se: 0.17, order: 1 }
  ].map(d => {
    const lo = beta - z * d.se, hi = beta + z * d.se;
    return { ...d, lo, hi, crossesZero: lo <= 0 && hi >= 0 };
  });
  return Plot.plot({
    width: Math.min(620, width),
    height: 240,
    marginLeft: 175,
    marginBottom: 46,
    marginTop: 20,
    style: { fontFamily: "'Inter', -apple-system, sans-serif", fontSize: "12px", color: "#1c1c1a", background: "transparent" },
    x: { label: "Program effect on HbA1c (95% CI, percentage points)", domain: [-0.7, 0.15], grid: true },
    y: { label: null, domain: [0.5, 4.5], ticks: [] },
    color: { domain: ["Excludes zero", "Crosses zero"], range: ["#2e6f40", "#b3000b"], legend: true },
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleX([0], { stroke: "#1c1c1a", strokeWidth: 1.5, strokeDasharray: "5,4" }),
      Plot.link(rows, {
        x1: "lo", x2: "hi", y: "order",
        stroke: d => d.crossesZero ? "Crosses zero" : "Excludes zero",
        strokeWidth: 3
      }),
      Plot.dot(rows, { x: () => beta, y: "order", fill: "#1c1c1a", r: 4.5 }),
      Plot.text(rows, {
        x: () => -0.7, y: "order", text: "label", textAnchor: "end", dx: -8,
        fill: "#1c1c1a", fontSize: 11, fontWeight: 600
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

::: {.aside}
Same point estimate ($\hat\beta = -0.30$ pp), four standard errors. The interval widens as each step admits more of the within-group correlation; <span style="color:#2e6f40;font-weight:700;">green</span> intervals exclude zero (significant), the <span style="color:#b3000b;font-weight:700;">red</span> region-clustered interval crosses zero (not significant). The defensible choice is vårdcentral clustering --- wide enough to be honest, with enough clusters to be trustworthy.
:::

Read the table top to bottom as a progressive admission of how much information we *really* have. The classical SE pretends all 8,000 patients are independent and screams significance. The robust SE fixes heteroskedasticity but still treats patients as independent. Clustering by vårdcentral --- the level treatment was actually assigned --- roughly doubles the SE and gives an honest, still-significant result. Clustering by region collapses us to 21 clusters: the SE balloons and the result loses significance --- but here the lesson is subtle. Because treatment varied *within* regions (center by center), region is *too coarse* a clustering level and throws away legitimate within-region variation; and with only 21 clusters the estimator is unreliable anyway. The defensible choice is **clustering by vårdcentral** --- the level of treatment assignment, with enough clusters to be trustworthy. The example shows both how much the SE depends on the clustering decision and why that decision must be reasoned, not mechanical.


<details>
<summary>Math Details: Deriving the Sandwich Estimator</summary>
<div class="details-content">

### Why the variance is a sandwich

Start from the OLS estimator written in matrix form. With $Y = X\beta + u$,

$$
\hat{\beta} = (X'X)^{-1} X' Y = (X'X)^{-1} X'(X\beta + u) = \beta + (X'X)^{-1} X' u.
$$

So the *estimation error* is $\hat{\beta} - \beta = (X'X)^{-1} X' u$. Taking the variance conditional on $X$ (treating $X$ as fixed) and using $\operatorname{Var}(Au) = A \operatorname{Var}(u) A'$ with $A = (X'X)^{-1}X'$:

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta} \mid X) = (X'X)^{-1} X' \,\underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(u \mid X)}_{\Omega}\, X (X'X)^{-1}.
$$

That is the sandwich exactly: bread $(X'X)^{-1}$, meat $X'\Omega X$, bread $(X'X)^{-1}$. No assumption about $\Omega$ has been used yet --- this expression is fully general.

### The homoskedastic collapse

If $\Omega = \sigma^2 I$ (homoskedasticity, no autocorrelation), the meat simplifies:

$$
X'\Omega X = X' (\sigma^2 I) X = \sigma^2 X'X,
$$

and substituting back,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta} \mid X) = (X'X)^{-1} \sigma^2 (X'X) (X'X)^{-1} = \sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1}.
$$

The classical formula is therefore not a separate result --- it is the sandwich evaluated at a spherical error covariance.

### Estimating the meat without knowing $\Omega$

Under heteroskedasticity, $\Omega = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \dots, \sigma_n^2)$. We cannot estimate $n$ separate variances from $n$ observations consistently *one at a time*, but White showed we do not need to: the *sum* $\sum_i \sigma_i^2 x_i x_i'$ is what enters the meat, and replacing each $\sigma_i^2$ with the squared residual $\hat{u}_i^2$ yields a consistent estimator of that sum:

$$
\widehat{X'\Omega X} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \hat{u}_i^2 \, x_i x_i'.
$$

The HC1 finite-sample correction multiplies this by $n/(n-k)$ to offset the fact that residuals are, on average, slightly smaller than the true errors (OLS fits the sample, deflating the residuals).

### The cluster generalization

If errors are correlated within groups, $\Omega$ is block-diagonal: a dense block for each cluster, zeros between clusters. The meat then sums *outer products of stacked cluster residuals* rather than individual squared residuals:

$$
\widehat{X'\Omega X} = \sum_{g=1}^{G} X_g' \,\hat{u}_g \hat{u}_g'\, X_g,
$$

which permits any within-cluster correlation pattern while imposing between-cluster independence. Consistency now requires $G \to \infty$ (many clusters), which is exactly why $G = 21$ regioner is dangerous.

</div>
</details>


## Code Implementations

The code below builds one synthetic Swedish primary-care dataset --- patients nested in vårdcentraler --- with deliberately heteroskedastic, within-center-correlated errors, then reports classical, robust (HC1), and cluster-robust standard errors side by side so you can see them diverge.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### R

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(sandwich)   # vcovHC, vcovCL
library(lmtest)     # coeftest

set.seed(2024)

# --- Build a clustered, heteroskedastic dataset ---
G    <- 60                      # vardcentraler (clusters)
nper <- 50                      # patients per center
center <- rep(1:G, each = nper)

# Center-level random effect -> within-center correlation
u_center  <- rnorm(G, sd = 0.6)[center]
program   <- rep(rbinom(G, 1, 0.5), each = nper)   # treatment assigned by CENTER
staffing  <- runif(G * nper, 2, 8)                  # nurses (individual-level)

# Heteroskedastic individual noise (sd grows with staffing)
u_ind <- rnorm(G * nper, sd = 1 + 0.4 * staffing)

# True model: program lowers HbA1c by 0.30; staffing by 0.10
hba1c <- 7.5 - 0.30 * program - 0.10 * staffing + u_center + u_ind

df <- tibble(hba1c, program, staffing, center = factor(center))

m <- lm(hba1c ~ program + staffing, data = df)

# 1) Classical (homoskedastic) SEs
classical <- coeftest(m)

# 2) Robust HC1 SEs
robust <- coeftest(m, vcov = vcovHC(m, type = "HC1"))

# 3) Cluster-robust SEs, clustered by vardcentral
clustered <- coeftest(m, vcov = vcovCL(m, cluster = ~ center))

cat("=== Classical ===\n");        print(classical)
cat("\n=== Robust (HC1) ===\n");    print(robust)
cat("\n=== Clustered by center ===\n"); print(clustered)

# Joint F-test: do program AND staffing jointly matter?
restricted <- lm(hba1c ~ 1, data = df)
anova(restricted, m)            # F-test, q = 2 restrictions

# --- Equivalent with fixest (handles clustering natively) ---
# library(fixest)
# feols(hba1c ~ program + staffing, data = df, vcov = "hetero")     # robust
# feols(hba1c ~ program + staffing, data = df, cluster = ~ center)  # clustered
```

### Python

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)

# --- Build a clustered, heteroskedastic dataset ---
G, nper = 60, 50                                  # 60 vardcentraler, 50 patients each
center  = np.repeat(np.arange(G), nper)

u_center = np.repeat(rng.normal(0, 0.6, G), nper)         # within-center correlation
program  = np.repeat(rng.binomial(1, 0.5, G), nper)       # treatment by CENTER
staffing = rng.uniform(2, 8, G * nper)                    # nurses (individual-level)

# Heteroskedastic individual noise (sd grows with staffing)
u_ind = rng.normal(0, 1 + 0.4 * staffing)

# True model: program -0.30, staffing -0.10
hba1c = 7.5 - 0.30 * program - 0.10 * staffing + u_center + u_ind

df = pd.DataFrame({"hba1c": hba1c, "program": program,
                   "staffing": staffing, "center": center})

m = smf.ols("hba1c ~ program + staffing", data=df).fit()

# 1) Classical (homoskedastic) SEs
classical = m

# 2) Robust HC1 SEs
robust = m.get_robustcov_results(cov_type="HC1")

# 3) Cluster-robust SEs, clustered by vardcentral
clustered = m.get_robustcov_results(cov_type="cluster",
                                    groups=df["center"])

for name, res in [("Classical", classical),
                  ("Robust (HC1)", robust),
                  ("Clustered by center", clustered)]:
    print(f"=== {name} ===")
    print(f"  program  beta={res.params[1]:+.3f}  SE={res.bse[1]:.3f}  t={res.tvalues[1]:+.2f}")
    print(f"  staffing beta={res.params[2]:+.3f}  SE={res.bse[2]:.3f}  t={res.tvalues[2]:+.2f}")

# Joint F-test: do program AND staffing jointly matter?
print("\nJoint F-test (program = staffing = 0):")
print(m.f_test("program = 0, staffing = 0"))
```

:::


## Exercises {.exercises}

::: {.exercise}
[Exercise 1]{.exercise-label}

A regression of HbA1c on vårdcentral staffing yields $\hat{\beta}_1 = -0.18$ with a classical standard error of $0.05$. The researcher then recomputes with robust (HC1) standard errors and gets $0.09$.

(a) Compute the classical and robust $t$-statistics for $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$.
(b) Compute the 95% confidence interval under each (use $t^{*} = 1.96$).
(c) The robust SE is nearly double the classical SE. What does that tell you about the data, and which SE should be reported?

<details>
<summary>Show answer</summary>
<div class="details-content">

**(a)** Classical: $t = -0.18 / 0.05 = -3.60$. Robust: $t = -0.18 / 0.09 = -2.00$.

**(b)** Classical CI: $-0.18 \pm 1.96 \times 0.05 = -0.18 \pm 0.098 = [-0.278, \, -0.082]$. Robust CI: $-0.18 \pm 1.96 \times 0.09 = -0.18 \pm 0.176 = [-0.356, \, -0.004]$. Both exclude zero, but the robust interval is much wider and only barely excludes zero.

**(c)** When the robust SE is much larger than the classical SE, there is substantial heteroskedasticity *correlated with the regressor* --- the classical formula was understating the uncertainty. The point estimate $-0.18$ is unaffected (OLS is unbiased either way), but the honest measure of precision is the robust one. **Report the robust SE.** The classical $t = -3.60$ projects false confidence; the truthful picture is the borderline robust $t = -2.00$.

</div>
</details>
:::

::: {.exercise}
[Exercise 2]{.exercise-label}

A diabetes coordination program is rolled out across 600 vårdcentraler in Sweden's 21 regioner; the analysis dataset has 8,000 patients. A colleague analyzes the data with classical (homoskedastic) standard errors and reports $t = -6.0$, declaring the program "highly significant."

(a) Why are the classical standard errors likely to be far too small here?
(b) At what level should the analyst cluster, and why?
(c) Why is clustering by region (21 clusters) potentially problematic even though region is "higher up" than vårdcentral?

<details>
<summary>Show answer</summary>
<div class="details-content">

**(a)** Patients at the same vårdcentral share an environment, staff, and protocols, so their error terms are *positively correlated within center*. Classical SEs assume all 8,000 observations are independent draws, which dramatically overstates the amount of independent information. The effective sample size is far closer to the number of centers (or regions) than to 8,000, so the classical SE --- and even the heteroskedasticity-robust SE, which still treats observations as independent --- is far too small and the $t = -6.0$ is inflated.

**(b)** Cluster at the **vårdcentral** level, because that is the level at which treatment was assigned and the level at which the within-group correlation lives. With about 600 clusters, the cluster-robust estimator is well behaved (many clusters), and it corrects the within-center correlation.

**(c)** Two reasons. First, treatment varied *within* regions (center by center), so clustering on region is *too coarse* --- it would discard legitimate within-region treatment variation and is not required to fix the correlation, which operates at the center level. Second, Sweden has only **21 regioner**, far below the 30--50-cluster rule of thumb. With so few clusters the cluster-robust SE is itself badly estimated and biased downward, so $t$-statistics remain unreliable. Vårdcentral clustering is the defensible choice; if region-level inference is genuinely needed, use a wild cluster bootstrap.

</div>
</details>
:::

::: {.exercise}
[Exercise 3]{.exercise-label}

A staffing model includes three coefficients: $\beta_{\text{läk}}$ (physicians), $\beta_{\text{ssk}}$ (district nurses), and $\beta_{\text{admin}}$ (admin staff). Individually, none is significant ($t = 1.1$, $0.9$, $1.3$). But the joint F-test for $H_0: \beta_{\text{läk}} = \beta_{\text{ssk}} = \beta_{\text{admin}} = 0$ gives $F = 5.8$ on $(3, 500)$ degrees of freedom, with $p = 0.0007$.

(a) How can three individually insignificant coefficients be jointly highly significant?
(b) What does the relationship $F = t^2$ tell you about testing a single coefficient, and why does it *not* apply here?

<details>
<summary>Show answer</summary>
<div class="details-content">

**(a)** The three staffing variables are highly **collinear** --- physicians, nurses, and admin staff tend to scale together across centers. Collinearity inflates each individual standard error, so each coefficient looks imprecise *on its own* (each variable's effect is hard to disentangle from the others'). But the F-test asks a different question: does the *group* of staffing variables jointly improve the fit? Because staffing as a bundle clearly matters, dropping all three together hurts the fit a lot, producing a large $F$. Individual t-tests and the joint F-test answer genuinely different questions; the joint test is the right one when the regressors move together.

**(b)** For a *single* restriction ($q = 1$), the F-statistic equals the square of the t-statistic: $F = t^2$. For example, $t = 2.0$ corresponds to $F = 4.0$, and the two tests give identical p-values. This identity holds only when you test **one** coefficient. Here the joint hypothesis imposes $q = 3$ restrictions, so $F \neq t^2$ for any single coefficient --- the F-test genuinely aggregates evidence across the three coefficients and cannot be reduced to any one t-test.

</div>
</details>
:::

::: {.exercise}
[Exercise 4]{.exercise-label}

Using the interactive coverage demo, set $\gamma = 1.4$ with **Classical** SEs and record the coverage (each setting runs 500 samples live; click **Reshuffle random draws** to confirm the number is stable). Then keep $\gamma = 1.4$, switch to **Robust (HC1)**, and read the coverage again. Finally return to **Classical**, keep $\gamma = 1.4$, and push $n$ to 200.

(a) What happens to coverage in each of the three runs?
(b) Explain why increasing $n$ does *not* rescue the classical CI, but switching to robust SEs does.

<details>
<summary>Show answer</summary>
<div class="details-content">

**(a)** Run 1 (classical, $\gamma = 1.4$, $n = 60$): coverage falls well below 95%, typically into the high 80s --- the CIs are too narrow and miss the true slope too often. Run 2 (robust, same $\gamma$ and $n$): coverage climbs back toward 95% --- the wider robust intervals catch the truth about 95% of the time. Run 3 (classical, $\gamma = 1.4$, $n = 200$): coverage stays stuck below 95%, roughly where it was at $n = 60$.

**(b)** The problem is not sampling noise in the SE --- it is a *wrong formula*. With heteroskedasticity, $\sigma^2(X'X)^{-1}$ is the wrong expression for $\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\beta})$, so the classical CI is miscalibrated *no matter how much data you collect*. More data shrinks the true sampling variance of $\hat{\beta}_1$ (the slope histogram tightens), but the classical SE shrinks at the wrong rate relative to that true variance, so the ratio stays off and coverage does not improve. The robust SE estimates the *correct* sandwich variance, so it widens the interval to exactly the right size regardless of $n$. The lesson: heteroskedasticity is a bias-in-the-SE problem, not a sample-size problem, and only the right estimator fixes it.

</div>
</details>
:::


::: {.key-takeaways}
**Key takeaways**

- $\hat\beta$ is a random variable; its standard error measures run-to-run spread, and a wrong SE poisons the $t$-statistic, p-value, and confidence interval all at once.
- The classical formula $\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ is the homoskedastic child of the sandwich; under heteroskedasticity it is usually too small, so OLS stays unbiased but inference breaks — and more data never fixes it.
- Report robust (HC1) standard errors by default: they are valid under heteroskedasticity and coincide with classical SEs when errors are homoskedastic, so there is essentially no downside.
- Cluster at the level treatment is assigned or where correlation lives; ignoring within-group correlation makes SEs far too small. But cluster-robust inference needs many clusters — Sweden's 21 regioner is too few.
- The F-test answers a joint question that several t-tests cannot; collinear regressors can be individually insignificant yet jointly significant. A correct standard error on a p-hacked specification is still a lie.
:::

## What Comes Next

We now have honest inference for a single cross-section: the right standard error, the right test, and a clear-eyed view of what clustering and p-hacking can do to a confidence interval. But the few-clusters problem flagged above --- and the within-group correlation that motivated clustering in the first place --- point toward data with a *repeated* structure: the same vårdcentraler or regioner observed over multiple years. That structure is not just a nuisance to cluster away; it is a powerful source of identification. The next module, [Panel Data](../05-panel/index.qmd), shows how observing the same units over time lets us difference away stubborn confounders that no cross-sectional regression could ever control for --- and how the clustering lessons of this page become essential to getting panel inference right.
